content.text: <ide_opened_file>The user opened the file /Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/results/axis1_boundary.json in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>

combine axis1 and axis3 scripts into 1 unified script for running evals on outputs. then, add functionality to run rollouts on the actual tasks to generate outputs, then add flags so I can specify which mode to run eg generate rollouts or preexisting outputs and multi judging runs for consistency or not, specific task or all,  which models for what etc.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	CONFIDENTIAL - DO NOT DISTRIBUTE
2	PHILO LABS
3	Research Engineer / Scientist Take-Home
4	Track R: Evaluating Rubrics for Multimodal Agentic RL
5	~4 hours | 2 calendar days | Jun 2026
6	Context
7	Math has formal proof. Code has unit tests. They are trustworthy because people built them on
8	purpose.
9	For knowledge work, creative work, and most tasks that matter, we have no such thing. We write
10	rubrics instead: short lists of criteria, each scored by a person or by a model acting as a judge.
11	The problem: a rubric can look like it grades quality while it really grades form. A memo can pass
12	every line and still say nothing. A strong analysis can fail lines that have nothing to do with whether it
13	is strong.
14	On a leaderboard, that is a small problem. As an RL reward, it compounds. The policy learns to
15	satisfy the checklist without doing the work, and the rubric quietly teaches the model the wrong thing.
16	So before we trust a rubric as training signal, we have to grade the rubric itself. This take-home asks
17	you to build that grader.
18	Why GDPval
19	You will work from GDPval (Patwardhan et al., 2025), OpenAI's open benchmark of real,
20	economically valuable knowledge work. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gdpval
21	•
22	•
23	•
24	•
25	Scale. 1,320 tasks across 44 occupations and 9 sectors. A gold subset of 220 is public.
26	The right shape. Tasks are multimodal. Many are exactly our kind: video understanding
27	(detectives, private investigators), video and image editing (film directors, VFX artists), plus
28	documents, slides, and spreadsheets.
29	What ships with each task. A prompt, reference files, a gold deliverable made by an
30	experienced professional, and a grading rubric.
31	Why that helps. The gold deliverable is a free oracle. It is exactly what you need to test
32	whether a rubric is calibrated.
33	The Task
34	Build two things and run them end to end on a slice of GDPval:
35	•
36	a rubric evaluator that scores how good a rubric is along the axes below, and
37	•
38	an automatic iterator that rewrites a rubric to raise those scores.
39	Use your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, or similar) heavily throughout.
40	Part 1: Get familiar with 10 tasks
41	We have chosen the 10 tasks for you. They are all video-centric on purpose. Video is where rubrics
42	are weakest, where we work, and the part candidates tend to avoid. Use these exact tasks. Do not
43	swap in document or slide tasks.
44	Occupation GDPval task folder
45	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__75401f7c
46	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8
47	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__c94452e4
48	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__e222075d
49	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__38889c3b
50	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__4b894ae3
51	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__ff85ee58
52	Private detectives and
53	investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2
54	Private detectives and
55	investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2
56	Producers and directors producers_and_directors__e4f664ea
57	The slice spans both capability families we care about:
58	•
59	•
60	Editing. Film and video editors, audio and video technicians, and producers and directors. The
61	output is an edited or produced video.
62	Video understanding. Private detectives and investigators. The work is to review footage and
63	find what matters.
64	Part 2: The rubric scorecard
65	This is the heart of the take-home, and the part we will read most closely. For each rubric, score it
66	along the axes below.
67	Treat them as a standing test suite. A rubric that fails any one is suspect, no matter how reasonable
68	it looks on the page. The list is not complete on purpose: finding the axes we have not named is part
69	of the work.
70	Axis 1: Boundary calibration
71	The cheap checks, run first. Two sanity anchors:
72	•
73	Gold scores near 1. Run the rubric on the gold deliverable.
74	•
75	Null scores near 0. Run it on a do-nothing output: an empty file, the unedited input, or a black
76	frame.
77	Axis 2: Reward hackability
78	This is the axis that matters most for RL. A rubric is hackable if a policy can raise its score without
79	improving the work. The policy will optimize for exactly what you reward, including the parts you did
80	not mean.
81	The classic trap is a criterion that only checks that something is present. Take a captioning rubric
82	with the line "the caption is visible."
83	A policy can satisfy it with a huge caption that covers the speaker's face. Visible, yes, but worse, not
84	better. The line states a presence condition with no matching absence condition.
85	The fix is to pair them: "the caption is visible AND does not cover the main subject." Presence plus
86	absence closes the gap.
87	That is one pattern. There are more, and we left them for you to find.
88	Axis 3: Consistency
89	A rubric is only useful if it returns the same verdict on the same work.
90	•
91	Self-consistency: run the same rubric on the same output several times with the same judge.
92	Do the scores reproduce, or wander?
93	•
94	Cross-model consistency: score the same output with two or more judge models. Do they
95	agree?
96	When two judges disagree, ask why. If one judge is simply more capable, fine. If a criterion is so
97	vague that two models read it differently, that is a rubric defect: fix the wording, do not blame the
98	judge.
99	And more (open on purpose)
100	We expect you to add axes we have not named. Strong candidates:
101	•
102	Discrimination: does it separate excellent work from merely adequate, not just good from
103	broken?
104	•
105	Coverage: does it grade what the task is actually about, or only the easy-to-name surface?
106	•
107	Saturation: how does it behave once every strong model passes it?
108	Part 3: The iterator
109	Build a loop that improves a rubric automatically:
110	•
111	evaluate the rubric on the axes above,
112	•
113	diagnose which axis it fails, and why,
114	•
115	rewrite the rubric to fix that failure,
116	•
117	evaluate again.
118	Run it on your 10 tasks. For each, report before and after: did hackability drop, did cross-model
119	agreement rise where it should, did the gold move toward 1 and the null toward 0?
120	Be frank about what moved and what did not. A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.
121	Part 4: Report
122	1,500-2,500 words in LaTeX, compiled to PDF with your coding agent. Cover:
123	•
124	the framing of your scorecard and your design choices,
125	•
126	what you learned running it on GDPval,
127	•
128	most importantly, an honest account of which rubrics you would trust as RL reward and which
129	you would not, and which axes you believe generalize to rubrics you never saw.
130	Deliverables
131	Deliverable Details
132	Report (PDF) LaTeX compiled to PDF via your coding agent. 1,500-2,500 words.
133	Code GitHub repo (preferred) or zip. Clean README with run instructions
134	and the agent prompts you used.
135	rubric_scorecard.tsv
136	Auto-generated by the pipeline. One row per (task x rubric version),
137	with a column for each axis (gold score, null score, hackability labels,
138	self-consistency variance, cross-model agreement) and a column
139	flagging suspected reward hacking. Reproducible with a single
140	command.
141	10 rubric packages For each task: the original rubric, the rewritten rubric, the test cases
142	used, and the hackability analysis. Released as one folder per task.
143	Agent session
144	Exported coding agent session. For Claude Code, run /export, or use
145	claude-code-transcripts to export as HTML. For other agents, export
146	the full session log per your tool's docs.
147	Evaluation
148	•
149	Systematic thinking (40%): a coherent framework, first-principles reasoning about what
150	makes a rubric trustworthy as training signal, and clear thinking about reward hacking and
151	about what a rubric fails to cover.
152	•
153	•
154	•
155	Rubric-evaluation quality (25%): axes that are precise, measurable, implementable from your
156	spec alone, and honest about which ones would survive being used as reward.
157	Implementation (20%): clean, modular, end to end. The evaluator and iterator run with a single
158	command, and the rubric packages are self-contained.
159	Agent fluency (15%): effective use of coding agents across the full workflow.
160	Logistics
161	•
162	•
163	•
164	•
165	Time: ~4 focused hours, 2 calendar days from receipt.
166	Resources: anything publicly available. The GDPval gold subset is on Hugging Face. API and
167	compute costs reimbursed up to $200 (note your spend).
168	Questions: email us. Good questions are a positive signal.
169	Submit: email a GitHub link or a zip containing all deliverables.
170	References
171	•
172	•
173	•
174	GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks.
175	Patwardhan et al., 2025 (OpenAI). The benchmark and gold task subset you will work from.
176	arXiv:2510.04374
177	Rubrics as Rewards: Reinforcement Learning Beyond Verifiable Domains. Gunjal et al.,
178	2025. Uses rubric feedback as an on-policy RL reward and studies how to turn rubric scores
179	into a reward. Precedent for treating rubrics as the reward signal. arXiv:2507.17746
180	AdvancedIF and RIFL: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and RL for Instruction Following. He
181	et al., 2025 (Meta Superintelligence Labs). An expert-curated rubric benchmark plus a pipeline
182	of rubric generation, a rubric verifier, and reward shaping. Precedent for the generator, verifier,
183	and updater shape. arXiv:2511.10507
184	This document is confidential. Do not share or post publicly.
185

Run it on your 10 tasks. For each, report before and after: did hackability drop, did cross-model
agreement rise where it should, did the gold move toward 1 and the null toward 0?
Be frank about what moved and what did not. A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.
Part 4: Report
1,500-2,500 words in LaTeX, compiled to PDF with your coding agent. Cover:
•
the framing of your scorecard and your design choices,
•
what you learned running it on GDPval,
•
most importantly, an honest account of which rubrics you would trust as RL reward and which
you would not, and which axes you believe generalize to rubrics you never saw.
Deliverables
Deliverable Details
Report (PDF) LaTeX compiled to PDF via your coding agent. 1,500-2,500 words.
Code GitHub repo (preferred) or zip. Clean README with run instructions
and the agent prompts you used.
rubric_scorecard.tsv
Auto-generated by the pipeline. One row per (task x rubric version),
with a column for each axis (gold score, null score, hackability labels,
self-consistency variance, cross-model agreement) and a column
flagging suspected reward hacking. Reproducible with a single
command.
10 rubric packages For each task: the original rubric, the rewritten rubric, the test cases
used, and the hackability analysis. Released as one folder per task.
Agent session
Exported coding agent session. For Claude Code, run /export, or use
claude-code-transcripts to export as HTML. For other agents, export
the full session log per your tool's docs.
Evaluation
•
Systematic thinking (40%): a coherent framework, first-principles reasoning about what
makes a rubric trustworthy as training signal, and clear thinking about reward hacking and
about what a rubric fails to cover.
•
•
•
Rubric-evaluation quality (25%): axes that are precise, measurable, implementable from your
spec alone, and honest about which ones would survive being used as reward.
Implementation (20%): clean, modular, end to end. The evaluator and iterator run with a single
command, and the rubric packages are self-contained.
Agent fluency (15%): effective use of coding agents across the full workflow.
Logistics
•
•
•
•
Time: ~4 focused hours, 2 calendar days from receipt.
Resources: anything publicly available. The GDPval gold subset is on Hugging Face. API and
compute costs reimbursed up to $200 (note your spend).
Questions: email us. Good questions are a positive signal.
Submit: email a GitHub link or a zip containing all deliverables.
References
•
•
•
GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks.
Patwardhan et al., 2025 (OpenAI). The benchmark and gold task subset you will work from.
arXiv:2510.04374
Rubrics as Rewards: Reinforcement Learning Beyond Verifiable Domains. Gunjal et al.,
2025. Uses rubric feedback as an on-policy RL reward and studies how to turn rubric scores
into a reward. Precedent for treating rubrics as the reward signal. arXiv:2507.17746
AdvancedIF and RIFL: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and RL for Instruction Following. He
et al., 2025 (Meta Superintelligence Labs). An expert-curated rubric benchmark plus a pipeline
of rubric generation, a rubric verifier, and reward shaping. Precedent for the generator, verifier,
and updater shape. arXiv:2511.10507
This document is confidential. Do not share or post publicly.

# Philo take-home — GDPval data + viewer

## Setup

```bash
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install pandas pyarrow requests
```

## Download the 10 target tasks

Fetches `openai/gdpval` from Hugging Face, filters to the 10 task folders
specified in `instructions.md` (Part 1), and writes each task's prompt,
rubric, reference files, and gold deliverable files into `data/<task_folder>/`.

```bash
python scripts/download_data.py
```

Re-running is safe/idempotent — it skips files that already exist on disk.

Output layout per task:

```
data/<occupation>__<task_short_id>/
  task.json           # prompt, sector, occupation, file lists
  rubric.json          # structured rubric items (score, criterion, tags, ids)
  rubric_pretty.txt     # human-readable rubric text as published
  reference_files/      # input files given to the worker
  deliverable_files/    # the gold deliverable (when released for that task)
data/manifest.json       # all 10 tasks' metadata in one file (used by the viewer)
```

## Browse the data in a UI

```bash
python scripts/serve.py
```

Opens `http://localhost:8765/viewer/index.html` — a sidebar lists the 10
tasks; each task has tabs for Prompt, Rubric (table + raw text), Reference
Files, and Gold Deliverable, with inline preview for video/audio/image/PDF
and a download link for other types (zip, docx, psd). The server supports
HTTP Range requests so large video files can be scrubbed/seeked.

7	"passes" are surfaced as leaks: they reward form/presence, not the work, and are
8	prime reward-hacking suspects (feeds Axis 2).

12	  python -m eval.axis1_boundary --tasks <folder> ...  # subset

25	from .rubric import load_rubric
26

28	
29	
30	def run(tasks, model_keys, verbose=False):

32	    rows = []  # for TSV

44	        print(f"  rubric items: {len(items)} | null variants: {list(nulls)}")
45	
46	        report[tf] = {}
47	        for m in models:
48	            gold_res = judge(m, gold, items, task["prompt"], temperature=0.0, verbose=verbose)

53

55	            gap = gold_res["normalized"] - worst_null
56	
57	            # leaks: positive items a NULL still earns >= threshold (form/presence rewards)
58	            leaks = []
59	            for it in items:

71	                  f"null_worst={worst_null:.2f} gap={gap:+.2f} leaks={len(leaks)}")
72	
73	            report[tf][m.key] = {
74	                "gold": gold_res, "nulls": null_res,
75	                "gap": gap, "leaks": leaks,
76	            }
77	            null_cols = {f"null_{v}": round(r["normalized"], 3) for v, r in null_res.items()}
78	            rows.append([
79	                tf, m.key, len(items),
80	                round(gold_res["normalized"], 3),
81	                round(gold_verif, 3),

84	                json.dumps(null_cols),

file.content:
"""Axis 1 — Boundary calibration.

Run each rubric on (a) the gold deliverable, expecting a normalized score near 1,
and (b) one or more synthesized do-nothing deliverables, expecting near 0.

A calibrated rubric shows a large gold-minus-null gap. Items that a NULL still
"passes" are surfaced as leaks: they reward form/presence, not the work, and are
prime reward-hacking suspects (feeds Axis 2).

Usage:
  python -m eval.axis1_boundary                       # all gold tasks, default judge
  python -m eval.axis1_boundary --tasks <folder> ...  # subset
  python -m eval.axis1_boundary --models gpt-mini qwen-vl
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import json

from . import config, null_inputs, run_common
from .config import CostTracker
from .deliverable import Bundle
from .harness import judge
from .models import resolve
from .rubric import load_rubric

LEAK_THRESHOLD = 0.5  # a null that earns >=50% of an item's points = leak


def run(tasks, model_keys, verbose=False):
    models = resolve(model_keys)
    rows = []  # for TSV
    report = {}

    for tf in tasks:
        if not run_common.has_gold(tf):
            print(f"skip {tf}: no gold deliverable released")
            continue
        print(f"\n=== {tf} ===")
        task = run_common.load_task(tf)
        items = load_rubric(tf)
        gold = run_common.gold_bundle(tf)
        nulls = null_inputs.build_nulls(tf)
        print(f"  rubric items: {len(items)} | null variants: {list(nulls)}")

        report[tf] = {}
        for m in models:
            gold_res = judge(m, gold, items, task["prompt"], temperature=0.0, verbose=verbose)
            null_res = {}
            for vname, vdir in nulls.items():
                b = run_common.null_bundle(tf, vname, vdir)
                null_res[vname] = judge(m, b, items, task["prompt"], temperature=0.0, verbose=verbose)

            worst_null = max((r["normalized"] for r in null_res.values()), default=0.0)
            gap = gold_res["normalized"] - worst_null

            # leaks: positive items a NULL still earns >= threshold (form/presence rewards)
            leaks = []
            for it in items:
                if it.max_score <= 0:
                    continue  # penalties aren't "passed" by doing nothing
                null_max = max((nr["per_item"][it.id]["fraction"] for nr in null_res.values()),
                               default=0.0)
                if null_max >= LEAK_THRESHOLD:
                    leaks.append({"id": it.id, "criterion": it.criterion,
                                  "null_fraction": round(null_max, 2)})

            gold_verif = gold_res.get("normalized_verifiable_only", gold_res["normalized"])
            print(f"  [{m.key}] gold={gold_res['normalized']:.2f} "
                  f"(verifiable-only={gold_verif:.2f}, unverifiable={gold_res.get('n_unverifiable', 0)}) "
                  f"null_worst={worst_null:.2f} gap={gap:+.2f} leaks={len(leaks)}")

            report[tf][m.key] = {
                "gold": gold_res, "nulls": null_res,
                "gap": gap, "leaks": leaks,
            }
            null_cols = {f"null_{v}": round(r["normalized"], 3) for v, r in null_res.items()}
            rows.append([
                tf, m.key, len(items),
                round(gold_res["normalized"], 3),
                round(gold_verif, 3),
                gold_res.get("n_unverifiable", 0),
                round(worst_null, 3), round(gap, 3), len(leaks),
                json.dumps(null_cols),
            ])

    out_json = config.RESULTS / "axis1_boundary.json"
    out_json.write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
    print(f"\nwrote {out_json.relative_to(config.ROOT)}")
    run_common.write_tsv(
        config.RESULTS / "axis1_boundary.tsv",
        ["task", "model", "n_items", "gold_score", "gold_verifiable_only",
         "n_unverifiable", "null_worst", "gap", "n_leaks", "null_breakdown"],
        rows,
    )
    print(CostTracker.summary())


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--tasks", nargs="*", default=None)
    ap.add_argument("--models", nargs="*", default=["gpt-mini"])
    ap.add_argument("--verbose", action="store_true")
    a = ap.parse_args()
    tasks = a.tasks or run_common.tasks_with_gold()
    run(tasks, a.models, a.verbose)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

1	"""Axis 3 — Consistency.
2	
3	Self-consistency: run the SAME judge on the SAME deliverable N times and measure
4	whether the verdict reproduces (low score spread, stable per-item calls).
5	
6	Cross-model consistency: run a panel of judges once each on the same deliverable
7	and measure agreement. Items where models disagree most are flagged as vague —
8	a rubric defect to fix in wording, not the judge's fault.
9	
10	By default both are run on the gold deliverable (the stable oracle).
11	
12	Usage:
13	  python -m eval.axis3_consistency                      # all gold tasks
14	  python -m eval.axis3_consistency --self-model qwen-vl --repeats 5
15	  python -m eval.axis3_consistency --panel gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
16	  python -m eval.axis3_consistency --on unedited_input  # consistency on a null instead
17	"""
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	import argparse
21	import json
22	import itertools
23	
24	from . import config, null_inputs, run_common
25	from .config import CostTracker
26	from .deliverable import Bundle
27	from .harness import judge
28	from .models import resolve
29	from .rubric import load_rubric, RubricItem
30	
31	
32	def _bundle_for(tf: str, which: str) -> Bundle:
33	    if which == "gold":
34	        return run_common.gold_bundle(tf)
35	    nulls = null_inputs.build_nulls(tf)
36	    if which not in nulls:
37	        raise SystemExit(f"{tf} has no null variant '{which}' (have: {list(nulls)})")
38	    return run_common.null_bundle(tf, which, nulls[which])
39	
40	
41	def _agreement(items: list[RubricItem], runs: list[dict]) -> dict:
42	    """Per-item dispersion across a set of scorecards (binarized at 0.5)."""
43	    per_item = []
44	    for it in items:
45	        fracs = [r["per_item"][it.id]["fraction"] for r in runs]
46	        binar = [1 if f >= 0.5 else 0 for f in fracs]
47	        unanimous = len(set(binar)) == 1
48	        per_item.append({
49	            "id": it.id, "criterion": it.criterion,
50	            "mean": round(sum(fracs) / len(fracs), 3),
51	            "stdev": round(run_common.stdev(fracs), 3),
52	            "unanimous": unanimous,
53	        })
54	    totals = [r["normalized"] for r in runs]
55	    frac_unanimous = sum(1 for p in per_item if p["unanimous"]) / len(per_item)
56	    return {
57	        "total_scores": [round(t, 3) for t in totals],
58	        "total_mean": round(sum(totals) / len(totals), 3),
59	        "total_stdev": round(run_common.stdev(totals), 3),
60	        "frac_items_unanimous": round(frac_unanimous, 3),
61	        "mean_item_stdev": round(sum(p["stdev"] for p in per_item) / len(per_item), 3),
62	        "most_contested": sorted(per_item, key=lambda p: -p["stdev"])[:8],
63	    }
64	
65	
66	def run(tasks, self_model, repeats, panel, which, temperature, verbose=False):
67	    sm = resolve([self_model])[0]
68	    panel_models = resolve(panel)
69	    report = {}
70	    self_rows, cross_rows = [], []
71	
72	    for tf in tasks:
73	        if not run_common.has_gold(tf) and which == "gold":
74	            print(f"skip {tf}: no gold"); continue
75	        print(f"\n=== {tf} (consistency on '{which}') ===")
76	        task = run_common.load_task(tf)
77	        items = load_rubric(tf)
78	        bundle = _bundle_for(tf, which)
79	        report[tf] = {}
80	
81	        # --- self-consistency ---
82	        print(f"  self-consistency: {sm.key} x{repeats} @ T={temperature}")
83	        runs = [judge(sm, bundle, items, task["prompt"], temperature=temperature, verbose=verbose)
84	                for _ in range(repeats)]
85	        self_ag = _agreement(items, runs)
86	        report[tf]["self"] = {"model": sm.key, "repeats": repeats, **self_ag}
87	        print(f"    scores={self_ag['total_scores']} stdev={self_ag['total_stdev']} "
88	              f"unanimous={self_ag['frac_items_unanimous']:.0%}")
89	        self_rows.append([tf, sm.key, repeats, self_ag["total_mean"],
90	                          self_ag["total_stdev"], self_ag["frac_items_unanimous"],
91	                          self_ag["mean_item_stdev"]])
92	
93	        # --- cross-model consistency ---
94	        print(f"  cross-model: {[m.key for m in panel_models]}")
95	        cross_runs = [judge(m, bundle, items, task["prompt"], temperature=0.0, verbose=verbose)
96	                      for m in panel_models]
97	        cross_ag = _agreement(items, cross_runs)
98	        # pairwise total-score spread
99	        scores = {m.key: r["normalized"] for m, r in zip(panel_models, cross_runs)}
100	        pair_gaps = {f"{a}-{b}": round(abs(scores[a] - scores[b]), 3)
101	                     for a, b in itertools.combinations(scores, 2)}
102	        report[tf]["cross"] = {"models": list(scores), "scores": scores,
103	                               "pairwise_gap": pair_gaps, **cross_ag}
104	        print(f"    scores={ {k: round(v,2) for k,v in scores.items()} } "
105	              f"stdev={cross_ag['total_stdev']} unanimous={cross_ag['frac_items_unanimous']:.0%}")
106	        cross_rows.append([tf, "+".join(scores), cross_ag["total_mean"],
107	                           cross_ag["total_stdev"], cross_ag["frac_items_unanimous"],
108	                           cross_ag["mean_item_stdev"], json.dumps({k: round(v,3) for k,v in scores.items()})])
109	
110	    (config.RESULTS / "axis3_consistency.json").write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
111	    print(f"\nwrote {(config.RESULTS / 'axis3_consistency.json').relative_to(config.ROOT)}")
112	    run_common.write_tsv(
113	        config.RESULTS / "axis3_self_consistency.tsv",
114	        ["task", "model", "repeats", "mean_score", "score_stdev", "frac_unanimous", "mean_item_stdev"],
115	        self_rows)
116	    run_common.write_tsv(
117	        config.RESULTS / "axis3_cross_model.tsv",
118	        ["task", "panel", "mean_score", "score_stdev", "frac_unanimous", "mean_item_stdev", "per_model_scores"],
119	        cross_rows)
120	    print(CostTracker.summary())
121	
122	
123	def main():
124	    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
125	    ap.add_argument("--tasks", nargs="*", default=None)
126	    ap.add_argument("--self-model", default="qwen-vl")
127	    ap.add_argument("--repeats", type=int, default=5)
128	    ap.add_argument("--panel", nargs="*", default=["gpt-mini", "qwen-vl", "gemini-lite"])
129	    ap.add_argument("--on", default="gold", help="which deliverable: gold | blank | unedited_input")
130	    ap.add_argument("--temperature", type=float, default=0.7)
131	    ap.add_argument("--verbose", action="store_true")
132	    a = ap.parse_args()
133	    tasks = a.tasks or run_common.tasks_with_gold()
134	    run(tasks, a.self_model, a.repeats, a.panel, a.on, a.temperature, a.verbose)
135	
136	
137	if __name__ == "__main__":
138	    main()
139

file.content:
"""Axis 3 — Consistency.

Self-consistency: run the SAME judge on the SAME deliverable N times and measure
whether the verdict reproduces (low score spread, stable per-item calls).

Cross-model consistency: run a panel of judges once each on the same deliverable
and measure agreement. Items where models disagree most are flagged as vague —
a rubric defect to fix in wording, not the judge's fault.

By default both are run on the gold deliverable (the stable oracle).

Usage:
  python -m eval.axis3_consistency                      # all gold tasks
  python -m eval.axis3_consistency --self-model qwen-vl --repeats 5
  python -m eval.axis3_consistency --panel gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
  python -m eval.axis3_consistency --on unedited_input  # consistency on a null instead
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import json
import itertools

from . import config, null_inputs, run_common
from .config import CostTracker
from .deliverable import Bundle
from .harness import judge
from .models import resolve
from .rubric import load_rubric, RubricItem


def _bundle_for(tf: str, which: str) -> Bundle:
    if which == "gold":
        return run_common.gold_bundle(tf)
    nulls = null_inputs.build_nulls(tf)
    if which not in nulls:
        raise SystemExit(f"{tf} has no null variant '{which}' (have: {list(nulls)})")
    return run_common.null_bundle(tf, which, nulls[which])


def _agreement(items: list[RubricItem], runs: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """Per-item dispersion across a set of scorecards (binarized at 0.5)."""
    per_item = []
    for it in items:
        fracs = [r["per_item"][it.id]["fraction"] for r in runs]
        binar = [1 if f >= 0.5 else 0 for f in fracs]
        unanimous = len(set(binar)) == 1
        per_item.append({
            "id": it.id, "criterion": it.criterion,
            "mean": round(sum(fracs) / len(fracs), 3),
            "stdev": round(run_common.stdev(fracs), 3),
            "unanimous": unanimous,
        })
    totals = [r["normalized"] for r in runs]
    frac_unanimous = sum(1 for p in per_item if p["unanimous"]) / len(per_item)
    return {
        "total_scores": [round(t, 3) for t in totals],
        "total_mean": round(sum(totals) / len(totals), 3),
        "total_stdev": round(run_common.stdev(totals), 3),
        "frac_items_unanimous": round(frac_unanimous, 3),
        "mean_item_stdev": round(sum(p["stdev"] for p in per_item) / len(per_item), 3),
        "most_contested": sorted(per_item, key=lambda p: -p["stdev"])[:8],
    }


def run(tasks, self_model, repeats, panel, which, temperature, verbose=False):
    sm = resolve([self_model])[0]
    panel_models = resolve(panel)
    report = {}
    self_rows, cross_rows = [], []

    for tf in tasks:
        if not run_common.has_gold(tf) and which == "gold":
            print(f"skip {tf}: no gold"); continue
        print(f"\n=== {tf} (consistency on '{which}') ===")
        task = run_common.load_task(tf)
        items = load_rubric(tf)
        bundle = _bundle_for(tf, which)
        report[tf] = {}

        # --- self-consistency ---
        print(f"  self-consistency: {sm.key} x{repeats} @ T={temperature}")
        runs = [judge(sm, bundle, items, task["prompt"], temperature=temperature, verbose=verbose)
                for _ in range(repeats)]
        self_ag = _agreement(items, runs)
        report[tf]["self"] = {"model": sm.key, "repeats": repeats, **self_ag}
        print(f"    scores={self_ag['total_scores']} stdev={self_ag['total_stdev']} "
              f"unanimous={self_ag['frac_items_unanimous']:.0%}")
        self_rows.append([tf, sm.key, repeats, self_ag["total_mean"],
                          self_ag["total_stdev"], self_ag["frac_items_unanimous"],
                          self_ag["mean_item_stdev"]])

        # --- cross-model consistency ---
        print(f"  cross-model: {[m.key for m in panel_models]}")
        cross_runs = [judge(m, bundle, items, task["prompt"], temperature=0.0, verbose=verbose)
                      for m in panel_models]
        cross_ag = _agreement(items, cross_runs)
        # pairwise total-score spread
        scores = {m.key: r["normalized"] for m, r in zip(panel_models, cross_runs)}
        pair_gaps = {f"{a}-{b}": round(abs(scores[a] - scores[b]), 3)
                     for a, b in itertools.combinations(scores, 2)}
        report[tf]["cross"] = {"models": list(scores), "scores": scores,
                               "pairwise_gap": pair_gaps, **cross_ag}
        print(f"    scores={ {k: round(v,2) for k,v in scores.items()} } "
              f"stdev={cross_ag['total_stdev']} unanimous={cross_ag['frac_items_unanimous']:.0%}")
        cross_rows.append([tf, "+".join(scores), cross_ag["total_mean"],
                           cross_ag["total_stdev"], cross_ag["frac_items_unanimous"],
                           cross_ag["mean_item_stdev"], json.dumps({k: round(v,3) for k,v in scores.items()})])

    (config.RESULTS / "axis3_consistency.json").write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
    print(f"\nwrote {(config.RESULTS / 'axis3_consistency.json').relative_to(config.ROOT)}")
    run_common.write_tsv(
        config.RESULTS / "axis3_self_consistency.tsv",
        ["task", "model", "repeats", "mean_score", "score_stdev", "frac_unanimous", "mean_item_stdev"],
        self_rows)
    run_common.write_tsv(
        config.RESULTS / "axis3_cross_model.tsv",
        ["task", "panel", "mean_score", "score_stdev", "frac_unanimous", "mean_item_stdev", "per_model_scores"],
        cross_rows)
    print(CostTracker.summary())


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--tasks", nargs="*", default=None)
    ap.add_argument("--self-model", default="qwen-vl")
    ap.add_argument("--repeats", type=int, default=5)
    ap.add_argument("--panel", nargs="*", default=["gpt-mini", "qwen-vl", "gemini-lite"])
    ap.add_argument("--on", default="gold", help="which deliverable: gold | blank | unedited_input")
    ap.add_argument("--temperature", type=float, default=0.7)
    ap.add_argument("--verbose", action="store_true")
    a = ap.parse_args()
    tasks = a.tasks or run_common.tasks_with_gold()
    run(tasks, a.self_model, a.repeats, a.panel, a.on, a.temperature, a.verbose)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

14

16	def has_gold(task_folder: str) -> bool:

24	def gold_bundle(task_folder: str) -> Bundle:
25	    return Bundle.with_references(
26	        "gold",
27	        config.DATA / task_folder / "deliverable_files",
28	        reference_dir(task_folder),
29	    )
30	
31	
32	def null_bundle(task_folder: str, variant: str, variant_dir: Path) -> Bundle:
33	    """Wrap a synthesized null deliverable with the same read-only reference files."""
34	    return Bundle.with_references(variant, variant_dir, reference_dir(task_folder))
35	
36	
37	def all_tasks() -> list[str]:
38	    return sorted(p.name for p in config.DATA.iterdir() if p.is_dir())
39	
40	
41	def tasks_with_gold() -> list[str]:
42	    return [t for t in all_tasks() if has_gold(t)]
43	
44	
45	def stdev(xs: list[float]) -> float:
46	    return statistics.pstdev(xs) if len(xs) > 1 else 0.0
47

49	def write_tsv(path: Path, header: list[str], rows: list[list]):
50	    with open(path, "w") as f:
51	        f.write("\t".join(header) + "\n")
52	        for r in rows:
53	            f.write("\t".join(str(x) for x in r) + "\n")
54	    print(f"  wrote {path.relative_to(config.ROOT)}  ({len(rows)} rows)")

1	"""The judge harness: one model grades one deliverable against one rubric.
2	
3	It is a small tool-calling agent. The model inspects the deliverable with the
4	modality tools it's allowed, then calls submit_scorecard. We normalize the
5	result to a [0,1] rubric score.
6	"""
7	from __future__ import annotations
8	
9	import json
10	
11	from . import llm, tools
12	from .deliverable import Bundle
13	from .models import Model
14	from .rubric import RubricItem, normalize
15	
16	MAX_ROUNDS = 12
17	MAX_TOOL_CALLS = 30          # hard budget across the whole judgement
18	MAX_CALLS_PER_TURN = 8       # ignore parallel-call floods within one turn
19	
20	SYSTEM = """You are a meticulous, skeptical grader. You score a work deliverable against a fixed rubric.
21	
22	Rules:
23	- Grade the files marked role=deliverable (the worker's output). Files marked
24	  role=reference are the INPUTS the worker was given; open them only to check
25	  comparison criteria (e.g. "matches the script"), never grade them as output.
26	- INSPECT before you score. Do not guess.
27	- Use the DETERMINISTIC tools for objective facts instead of eyeballing:
28	  * file_facts  -> extension/type, PDF page count, word count (e.g. "is a PDF",
29	                   "<= 2 pages", word limits)
30	  * probe_media -> codec, exact resolution, fps, runtime in seconds (e.g.
31	                   "H.264", "1920x1080", "runtime 29.9-30.1s", "music fits 30s")
32	- For visual criteria ("includes a shot of X", identifiable faces, on-screen
33	  text), call view_video_frames; it returns one frame per scene cut. To read a
34	  text card exactly, re-sample that time window with start_seconds/end_seconds.
35	- For anything about sound (music style, tone, mix, audible VO) you MUST consult
36	  the audio: listen_audio if you have it, otherwise transcribe_audio. Do not score
37	  audio criteria from visuals alone.
38	- Judge ONLY what the rubric asks. Give each item a verdict:
39	  * met          criterion clearly TRUE of the deliverable (full points)
40	  * unmet        criterion clearly FALSE (0)
41	  * partial      genuinely in between (give `awarded` in [0,1])
42	  * unverifiable the criterion cannot be checked from the deliverable or ANY tool
43	                 (external provenance like "footage is royalty-free / from Pexels",
44	                 "music from a stock provider") and there is no source log in the
45	                 deliverable. Use it for exactly these cases — not as a cop-out.
46	- PENALTY items have negative points and name a bad thing: 'met' means the bad
47	  thing IS present (incurs the penalty). A clean gold deliverable is 'unmet' on penalties.
48	- Be strict: a heading being present is not the same as it being correct.
49	- A blank / do-nothing deliverable should be 'unmet' on almost everything.
50	- When done, call submit_scorecard exactly once with one entry per rubric item id."""
51	
52	
53	def _rubric_block(items: list[RubricItem]) -> str:
54	    lines = []
55	    for it in items:
56	        tag = "PENALTY " if it.max_score < 0 else ""
57	        lines.append(f"[{it.id}] ({tag}{it.max_score:g} pts) {it.criterion}")
58	    return "\n".join(lines)
59	
60	
61	def judge(
62	    model: Model,
63	    bundle: Bundle,
64	    items: list[RubricItem],
65	    task_prompt: str,
66	    temperature: float = 0.0,
67	    verbose: bool = False,
68	) -> dict:
69	    """Run the agent loop. Returns normalize(...) dict plus raw notes/meta."""
70	    listing = "\n".join(f"- {f['name']} [role={f['role']}, {f['type']}, {f['size_kb']}KB]"
71	                        for f in bundle.listing()) or "(empty)"
72	    user = (
73	        f"TASK GIVEN TO THE WORKER:\n{task_prompt.strip()}\n\n"
74	        f"DELIVERABLE TO GRADE: bundle '{bundle.label}'. "
75	        f"It contains EXACTLY these files (use these names verbatim):\n{listing}\n\n"
76	        f"Inspect them with the tools, then grade every rubric item below.\n\n"
77	        f"RUBRIC ({len(items)} items):\n{_rubric_block(items)}"
78	    )
79	    messages = [
80	        {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM},
81	        {"role": "user", "content": user},
82	    ]
83	    schemas = tools.tool_schemas(model)
84	
85	    awarded: dict[str, float] = {}
86	    unverifiable: set[str] = set()
87	    notes: dict[str, str] = {}
88	    seen: dict[str, str] = {}     # tool-call signature -> prior text (dedupe loops)
89	    rounds = 0
90	    tool_calls_used = 0
91	    submitted = False
92	
93	    while rounds < MAX_ROUNDS and not submitted:
94	        rounds += 1
95	        # last chance, or out of inspection budget: force the scorecard
96	        force = rounds == MAX_ROUNDS or tool_calls_used >= MAX_TOOL_CALLS
97	        msg = llm.chat(
98	            model, messages, tools=schemas,
99	            tool_choice=({"type": "function", "function": {"name": "submit_scorecard"}}
100	                         if force else "auto"),
101	            temperature=temperature, max_tokens=4096,
102	        )
103	        tcs = msg.tool_calls or []
104	        # Record the assistant turn (with tool calls) verbatim.
105	        messages.append({
106	            "role": "assistant",
107	            "content": msg.content or "",
108	            "tool_calls": [
109	                {"id": tc.id, "type": "function",
110	                 "function": {"name": tc.function.name, "arguments": tc.function.arguments}}
111	                for tc in tcs
112	            ] or None,
113	        })
114	        if not tcs:
115	            # model answered in prose; nudge it to use the tool
116	            messages.append({"role": "user",
117	                             "content": "Call submit_scorecard now with one entry per item id."})
118	            continue
119	
120	        media_followups: list[dict] = []
121	        for i, tc in enumerate(tcs):
122	            try:
123	                args = json.loads(tc.function.arguments or "{}")
124	            except json.JSONDecodeError:
125	                args = {}
126	            # Cap parallel-call floods: answer the first few, refuse the rest.
127	            if tc.function.name != "submit_scorecard" and i >= MAX_CALLS_PER_TURN:
128	                messages.append({"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": tc.id,
129	                                 "content": "skipped: too many tool calls in one turn"})
130	                continue
131	            if tc.function.name != "submit_scorecard":
132	                tool_calls_used += 1
133	                sig = tc.function.name + "|" + json.dumps(args, sort_keys=True)
134	                if sig in seen:
135	                    # Repeat call: acknowledge without re-attaching media (saves tokens, breaks loops).
136	                    messages.append({"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": tc.id,
137	                                     "content": "(already provided earlier) " + seen[sig][:200]})
138	                    continue
139	            if tc.function.name == "submit_scorecard":
140	                for e in args.get("scores", []):
141	                    if "id" not in e:
142	                        continue
143	                    iid = e["id"]
144	                    verdict = (e.get("verdict") or "").lower()
145	                    if verdict == "met":
146	                        awarded[iid] = 1.0
147	                    elif verdict == "unmet":
148	                        awarded[iid] = 0.0
149	                    elif verdict == "unverifiable":
150	                        awarded[iid] = 0.0
151	                        unverifiable.add(iid)
152	                    else:  # partial, or a model that ignored the enum
153	                        awarded[iid] = float(e.get("awarded", 0.0))
154	                    if e.get("note"):
155	                        notes[iid] = e["note"]
156	                messages.append({"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": tc.id,
157	                                 "content": "scorecard received"})
158	                submitted = True
159	                continue
160	            text, parts = tools.dispatch(tc.function.name, args, bundle)
161	            seen[sig] = text
162	            if verbose:
163	                print(f"  [{model.key}] {tc.function.name}({args}) -> {text[:60]!r}")
164	            messages.append({"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": tc.id, "content": text})
165	            media_followups.extend(parts)
166	
167	        if media_followups and not submitted:
168	            messages.append({"role": "user", "content":
169	                             [{"type": "text", "text": "Attached media from your tool call(s):"},
170	                              *media_followups]})
171	
172	    result = normalize(items, awarded, unverifiable)
173	    result["notes"] = notes
174	    result["unverifiable"] = sorted(unverifiable)
175	    result["rounds"] = rounds
176	    result["submitted"] = submitted
177	    result["model"] = model.key
178	    result["n_items_scored"] = len(awarded)
179	    return result

"""The judge harness: one model grades one deliverable against one rubric.

It is a small tool-calling agent. The model inspects the deliverable with the
modality tools it's allowed, then calls submit_scorecard. We normalize the
result to a [0,1] rubric score.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json

from . import llm, tools
from .deliverable import Bundle
from .models import Model
from .rubric import RubricItem, normalize

MAX_ROUNDS = 12
MAX_TOOL_CALLS = 30          # hard budget across the whole judgement
MAX_CALLS_PER_TURN = 8       # ignore parallel-call floods within one turn

SYSTEM = """You are a meticulous, skeptical grader. You score a work deliverable against a fixed rubric.

Rules:
- Grade the files marked role=deliverable (the worker's output). Files marked
  role=reference are the INPUTS the worker was given; open them only to check
  comparison criteria (e.g. "matches the script"), never grade them as output.
- INSPECT before you score. Do not guess.
- Use the DETERMINISTIC tools for objective facts instead of eyeballing:
  * file_facts  -> extension/type, PDF page count, word count (e.g. "is a PDF",
                   "<= 2 pages", word limits)
  * probe_media -> codec, exact resolution, fps, runtime in seconds (e.g.
                   "H.264", "1920x1080", "runtime 29.9-30.1s", "music fits 30s")
- For visual criteria ("includes a shot of X", identifiable faces, on-screen
  text), call view_video_frames; it returns one frame per scene cut. To read a
  text card exactly, re-sample that time window with start_seconds/end_seconds.
- For anything about sound (music style, tone, mix, audible VO) you MUST consult
  the audio: listen_audio if you have it, otherwise transcribe_audio. Do not score
  audio criteria from visuals alone.
- Judge ONLY what the rubric asks. Give each item a verdict:
  * met          criterion clearly TRUE of the deliverable (full points)
  * unmet        criterion clearly FALSE (0)
  * partial      genuinely in between (give `awarded` in [0,1])
  * unverifiable the criterion cannot be checked from the deliverable or ANY tool
                 (external provenance like "footage is royalty-free / from Pexels",
                 "music from a stock provider") and there is no source log in the
                 deliverable. Use it for exactly these cases — not as a cop-out.
- PENALTY items have negative points and name a bad thing: 'met' means the bad
  thing IS present (incurs the penalty). A clean gold deliverable is 'unmet' on penalties.
- Be strict: a heading being present is not the same as it being correct.
- A blank / do-nothing deliverable should be 'unmet' on almost everything.
- When done, call submit_scorecard exactly once with one entry per rubric item id."""


def _rubric_block(items: list[RubricItem]) -> str:
    lines = []
    for it in items:
        tag = "PENALTY " if it.max_score < 0 else ""
        lines.append(f"[{it.id}] ({tag}{it.max_score:g} pts) {it.criterion}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


def judge(
    model: Model,
    bundle: Bundle,
    items: list[RubricItem],
    task_prompt: str,
    temperature: float = 0.0,
    verbose: bool = False,
) -> dict:
    """Run the agent loop. Returns normalize(...) dict plus raw notes/meta."""
    listing = "\n".join(f"- {f['name']} [role={f['role']}, {f['type']}, {f['size_kb']}KB]"
                        for f in bundle.listing()) or "(empty)"
    user = (
        f"TASK GIVEN TO THE WORKER:\n{task_prompt.strip()}\n\n"
        f"DELIVERABLE TO GRADE: bundle '{bundle.label}'. "
        f"It contains EXACTLY these files (use these names verbatim):\n{listing}\n\n"
        f"Inspect them with the tools, then grade every rubric item below.\n\n"
        f"RUBRIC ({len(items)} items):\n{_rubric_block(items)}"
    )
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM},
        {"role": "user", "content": user},
    ]
    schemas = tools.tool_schemas(model)

    awarded: dict[str, float] = {}
    unverifiable: set[str] = set()
    notes: dict[str, str] = {}
    seen: dict[str, str] = {}     # tool-call signature -> prior text (dedupe loops)
    rounds = 0
    tool_calls_used = 0
    submitted = False

    while rounds < MAX_ROUNDS and not submitted:
        rounds += 1
        # last chance, or out of inspection budget: force the scorecard
        force = rounds == MAX_ROUNDS or tool_calls_used >= MAX_TOOL_CALLS
        msg = llm.chat(
            model, messages, tools=schemas,
            tool_choice=({"type": "function", "function": {"name": "submit_scorecard"}}
                         if force else "auto"),
            temperature=temperature, max_tokens=4096,
        )
        tcs = msg.tool_calls or []
        # Record the assistant turn (with tool calls) verbatim.
        messages.append({
            "role": "assistant",
            "content": msg.content or "",
            "tool_calls": [
                {"id": tc.id, "type": "function",
                 "function": {"name": tc.function.name, "arguments": tc.function.arguments}}
                for tc in tcs
            ] or None,
        })
        if not tcs:
            # model answered in prose; nudge it to use the tool
            messages.append({"role": "user",
                             "content": "Call submit_scorecard now with one entry per item id."})
            continue

        media_followups: list[dict] = []
        for i, tc in enumerate(tcs):
            try:
                args = json.loads(tc.function.arguments or "{}")
            except json.JSONDecodeError:
                args = {}
            # Cap parallel-call floods: answer the first few, refuse the rest.
            if tc.function.name != "submit_scorecard" and i >= MAX_CALLS_PER_TURN:
                messages.append({"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": tc.id,
                                 "content": "skipped: too many tool calls in one turn"})
                continue
            if tc.function.name != "submit_scorecard":
                tool_calls_used += 1
                sig = tc.function.name + "|" + json.dumps(args, sort_keys=True)
                if sig in seen:
                    # Repeat call: acknowledge without re-attaching media (saves tokens, breaks loops).
                    messages.append({"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": tc.id,
                                     "content": "(already provided earlier) " + seen[sig][:200]})
                    continue
            if tc.function.name == "submit_scorecard":
                for e in args.get("scores", []):
                    if "id" not in e:
                        continue
                    iid = e["id"]
                    verdict = (e.get("verdict") or "").lower()
                    if verdict == "met":
                        awarded[iid] = 1.0
                    elif verdict == "unmet":
                        awarded[iid] = 0.0
                    elif verdict == "unverifiable":
                        awarded[iid] = 0.0
                        unverifiable.add(iid)
                    else:  # partial, or a model that ignored the enum
                        awarded[iid] = float(e.get("awarded", 0.0))
                    if e.get("note"):
                        notes[iid] = e["note"]
                messages.append({"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": tc.id,
                                 "content": "scorecard received"})
                submitted = True
                continue
            text, parts = tools.dispatch(tc.function.name, args, bundle)
            seen[sig] = text
            if verbose:
                print(f"  [{model.key}] {tc.function.name}({args}) -> {text[:60]!r}")
            messages.append({"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": tc.id, "content": text})
            media_followups.extend(parts)

        if media_followups and not submitted:
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content":
                             [{"type": "text", "text": "Attached media from your tool call(s):"},
                              *media_followups]})

    result = normalize(items, awarded, unverifiable)
    result["notes"] = notes
    result["unverifiable"] = sorted(unverifiable)
    result["rounds"] = rounds
    result["submitted"] = submitted
    result["model"] = model.key
    result["n_items_scored"] = len(awarded)
    return result

content.content:
1	"""Judge-model registry.
2	
3	The suite is chosen to vary three things at once, cheaply:
4	
5	  * provider          OpenAI vs OpenRouter  -> genuine cross-vendor consistency check
6	  * capability tier   nano/mini, small/large MoE -> "does a stronger judge agree?"
7	  * modality access   text / +image / +audio -> the axis the take-home calls out
8	
9	Prices are USD per *token* (not per million) so CostTracker can multiply directly.
10	They are rough catalogue figures (June 2026); update if billing changes.
11	
12	Modalities the harness understands:
13	  "text"  - always; reads extracted text / transcripts
14	  "image" - can see rendered PDF pages, photos, and sampled video frames
15	  "audio" - can natively listen to an attached audio clip (vs. reading a transcript)
16	
17	A model that lacks "audio" still gets at audio *content* through the
18	transcribe_audio tool (Whisper). That degraded path is exactly what makes the
19	modality axis measurable: native-audio judges vs. transcript-only judges scoring
20	the same deliverable.
21	"""
22	from __future__ import annotations
23	
24	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
25	from typing import Set
26	
27	from . import config
28	
29	
30	@dataclass(frozen=True)

"""Judge-model registry.

The suite is chosen to vary three things at once, cheaply:

  * provider          OpenAI vs OpenRouter  -> genuine cross-vendor consistency check
  * capability tier   nano/mini, small/large MoE -> "does a stronger judge agree?"
  * modality access   text / +image / +audio -> the axis the take-home calls out

Prices are USD per *token* (not per million) so CostTracker can multiply directly.
They are rough catalogue figures (June 2026); update if billing changes.

Modalities the harness understands:
  "text"  - always; reads extracted text / transcripts
  "image" - can see rendered PDF pages, photos, and sampled video frames
  "audio" - can natively listen to an attached audio clip (vs. reading a transcript)

A model that lacks "audio" still gets at audio *content* through the
transcribe_audio tool (Whisper). That degraded path is exactly what makes the
modality axis measurable: native-audio judges vs. transcript-only judges scoring
the same deliverable.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Set

from . import config


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Model:
    key: str                      # short handle used on the CLI / in result files
    provider: str                 # "openai" | "openrouter"
    model_id: str                 # provider-native id
    modalities: Set[str]
    tier: str                     # human label: strong / mid / weak / free
    in_price: float = 0.0         # $ per prompt token
    out_price: float = 0.0        # $ per completion token

    @property
    def is_reasoning(self) -> bool:
        # GPT-5.x reasoning models ignore temperature and want max_completion_tokens.
        return self.provider == "openai" and self.model_id.startswith("gpt-5")

    def supports(self, modality: str) -> bool:
        return modality in self.modalities


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# The suite. Keep it small: every model here earns its place on an axis.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
REGISTRY = {
    # --- OpenAI vision-tier, two capability rungs (cheap capability ablation) ---
    "gpt-mini": Model(
        "gpt-mini", "openai", "gpt-5.4-mini", {"text", "image"}, "strong",
        in_price=0.25e-6, out_price=2.0e-6,
    ),
    "gpt-nano": Model(
        "gpt-nano", "openai", "gpt-5.4-nano", {"text", "image"}, "weak",
        in_price=0.05e-6, out_price=0.40e-6,
    ),

    # --- Qwen, same family, with vs. without vision: the modality ablation pair ---
    "qwen-vl": Model(
        "qwen-vl", "openrouter", "qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b", {"text", "image"}, "mid",
        in_price=0.14e-6, out_price=0.55e-6,
    ),
    "qwen-text": Model(
        "qwen-text", "openrouter", "qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct-2507", {"text"}, "mid",
        in_price=0.05e-6, out_price=0.20e-6,
    ),

    # --- Full-modality judges: the only ones that natively HEAR audio ---
    "gemini-lite": Model(
        "gemini-lite", "openrouter", "google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite",
        {"text", "image", "audio"}, "mid",
        in_price=0.10e-6, out_price=0.40e-6,
    ),
    "nemotron-omni": Model(   # free; second native-audio judge for cross-model on A/V tasks
        "nemotron-omni", "openrouter", "nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni-30b-a3b-reasoning:free",
        {"text", "image", "audio"}, "free",
        in_price=0.0, out_price=0.0,
    ),
}

# Sensible defaults if the caller doesn't pass --models.
# Cross-model panel: one per provider/modality corner, all cheap.
DEFAULT_CROSS_MODEL = ["gpt-mini", "qwen-vl", "gemini-lite"]
# Self-consistency: cheapest capable judge, repeated.
DEFAULT_SELF = "qwen-vl"
# Modality-ablation triple, for the "access to modalities" experiment.
MODALITY_LADDER = ["qwen-text", "qwen-vl", "gemini-lite"]


def resolve(keys) -> list[Model]:
    out = []
    for k in keys:
        if k not in REGISTRY:
            raise SystemExit(f"unknown model '{k}'. known: {', '.join(REGISTRY)}")
        out.append(REGISTRY[k])
    return out

file.content:
"""Shared paths, env loading, and cost tracking for the rubric-grader eval suite."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import threading
from pathlib import Path

ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
DATA = ROOT / "data"
RESULTS = ROOT / "eval" / "results"
NULLS = ROOT / "eval" / "null_inputs"
CACHE = ROOT / "eval" / ".cache"  # transcripts, extracted frames, etc.

for _d in (RESULTS, NULLS, CACHE):
    _d.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)


def _load_env() -> None:
    """Minimal .env loader (avoids a python-dotenv dependency)."""
    env = ROOT / ".env"
    if not env.exists():
        return
    for line in env.read_text().splitlines():
        line = line.strip()
        if not line or line.startswith("#") or "=" not in line:
            continue
        k, v = line.split("=", 1)
        os.environ.setdefault(k.strip(), v.strip())


_load_env()

OPENAI_API_KEY = os.environ.get("OPENAI_API_KEY", "")
OPENROUTER_API_KEY = os.environ.get("OPENROUTER_API_KEY", "")


class CostTracker:
    """Cheap, approximate spend tracker so a run never silently burns credits."""

    _lock = threading.Lock()
    prompt_tokens = 0
    completion_tokens = 0
    usd = 0.0
    calls = 0

    @classmethod
    def add(cls, prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int, in_price: float, out_price: float) -> None:
        with cls._lock:
            cls.prompt_tokens += prompt_tokens
            cls.completion_tokens += completion_tokens
            cls.usd += prompt_tokens * in_price + completion_tokens * out_price
            cls.calls += 1

    @classmethod
    def summary(cls) -> str:
        return (
            f"[cost] calls={cls.calls} "
            f"prompt={cls.prompt_tokens:,} completion={cls.completion_tokens:,} "
            f"~${cls.usd:.4f}"
        )

1	# Rubric scorecard — Axis 1 & Axis 3
2	
3	Scripts that grade GDPval rubrics along **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)** and
4	**Axis 3 (consistency)** from the take-home spec, plus the judge harness, model
5	suite, and null-input generator they depend on.
6	
7	```
8	eval/
9	  models.py            # judge-model registry (provider × capability × modality)
10	  llm.py               # OpenAI-compatible client (OpenAI + OpenRouter), cost tracking
11	  media.py             # ffmpeg frames / audio clips + Whisper transcription (cached)
12	  deliverable.py       # Bundle: flat file view over a deliverable, incl. zip members
13	  tools.py             # agent tools, gated by each model's modalities
14	  harness.py           # the judge: tool-using agent -> per-item [0,1] scorecard
15	  rubric.py            # rubric loading + normalization (handles penalty items)
16	  null_inputs.py       # generates do-nothing deliverables per task
17	  axis1_boundary.py    # Axis 1 runner
18	  axis3_consistency.py # Axis 3 runner
19	  results/             # *.json (full detail) + *.tsv (scorecard rows)
20	```
21	
22	## Setup
23	
24	```bash
25	source .venv/bin/activate
26	pip install openai pymupdf pillow        # plus the existing pandas/pyarrow/requests
27	brew install ffmpeg                       # frames, audio extraction
28	# .env must define OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENROUTER_API_KEY
29	python scripts/download_data.py          # if data/ isn't populated yet
30	```
31	
32	## The judge suite (`models.py`)
33	
34	Chosen cheap, and to vary three things so the axes are measurable:
35	
36	| key | provider | model | modalities | role on the axes |
37	|-----|----------|-------|-----------|------------------|
38	| `gpt-mini` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-mini | text, image | strong vision anchor; cross-vendor |
39	| `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-nano | text, image | weak rung → capability ablation vs `gpt-mini` |
40	| `qwen-vl` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text, image | cheap mid judge; cross-vendor |
41	| `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text | **modality ablation**: same family, no vision |
42	| `gemini-lite` | OpenRouter | google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text, image, **audio** | the only judge that natively *hears* |
43	| `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text, image, audio | free 2nd native-audio judge for A/V tasks |
44	
45	A judge without `audio` still reaches audio *content* through the
46	`transcribe_audio` tool (Whisper). Native-audio vs transcript-only on the same
47	deliverable is exactly the **modality-access** experiment. The
48	`MODALITY_LADDER = [qwen-text, qwen-vl, gemini-lite]` is the text → +vision →
49	+audio triple for that ablation.
50	
51	## The harness (`harness.py`)
52	
53	One model grades one deliverable against one rubric as a small tool-calling
54	agent. It is handed the file list (deliverable files plus read-only `reference`
55	inputs for comparison criteria), inspects with the tools its modalities allow,
56	then calls `submit_scorecard` with a per-item **verdict**:
57	`met | unmet | partial | unverifiable`. Safeguards: per-judgement tool-call
58	budget, per-turn flood cap, duplicate-call dedupe, forced scorecard on the last round.
59	
60	To give the judge a *fair shot* (early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to
61	things it couldn't see, vs ~3% genuine gaps), the toolbox includes deterministic
62	fact tools so objective criteria are never guessed:
63	
64	* `probe_media` — ffprobe ground truth: codec, exact resolution, fps, runtime,
65	  audio channels (kills "cannot determine codec/resolution/runtime").
66	* `file_facts` — extension/type, PDF page count, word count ("is a PDF", "<= 2 pages").
67	* `view_video_frames` — defaults to **one frame per scene cut** (distinct shots,
68	  for "includes a shot of X" / identifiable-face checks); pass `start/end` to
69	  re-sample a window at higher resolution to read on-screen text.
70	* read-only **reference files**, so "matches the script" items can compare.
71	* an explicit **`unverifiable`** verdict for criteria no judge can check from the
72	  artifact (external provenance like "footage is royalty-free / from Pexels").
73	
74	Scores are normalized by the **positive ceiling** (sum of positive item points);
75	**penalty items** (negative points, e.g. "uses footage with identifiable faces:
76	-85") subtract when `met`, flooring at 0. Two scores are reported: `normalized`
77	(unverifiable items = 0) and `normalized_verifiable_only` (unverifiable items
78	dropped from numerator and denominator — the fair score given what is checkable).
79	A large gap between them is itself a rubric **verifiability defect**.
80	
81	## Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)
82	
83	Per task, keyed on the gold deliverable's type:
84	
85	| gold type | `blank` | `unedited_input` |
86	|-----------|---------|------------------|
87	| PDF | one empty page | the unrevised source doc rendered to PDF |
88	| video (mp4) | 5 s black + silence | source audio over a black frame |
89	| archive (stems zip) | zip of 5 s silence | the raw input audio re-zipped, unmixed |
90	
91	```bash
92	python -m eval.null_inputs          # (re)generate all; writes eval/null_inputs/<task>/<variant>/
93	```
94	
95	## Axis 1 — boundary calibration
96	
97	Runs the rubric on gold (expect ≈1) and on each null (expect ≈0). Reports the
98	gold−null **gap** and **leaks**: positive items a do-nothing output still passes
99	(form/presence criteria — reward-hacking suspects, feeds Axis 2).
100	
101	```bash
102	python -m eval.axis1_boundary                          # all gold tasks, judge=gpt-mini
103	python -m eval.axis1_boundary --models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
104	python -m eval.axis1_boundary --tasks <folder> --verbose
105	```
106	Outputs: `results/axis1_boundary.{json,tsv}` (cols: task, model, n_items,
107	gold_score, gold_verifiable_only, n_unverifiable, null_worst, gap, n_leaks,
108	null_breakdown).
109	
110	## Axis 3 — consistency
111	
112	* **Self-consistency**: one judge, same deliverable, `--repeats` times at
113	  `--temperature` → score stdev + fraction of items with a unanimous binary call.
114	* **Cross-model**: a `--panel` of judges, once each → score spread, pairwise
115	  gaps, and the most-contested items (vague criteria to reword).
116	
117	```bash
118	python -m eval.axis3_consistency                                   # defaults
119	python -m eval.axis3_consistency --self-model qwen-vl --repeats 5 \
120	    --panel gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
121	python -m eval.axis3_consistency --on unedited_input               # consistency on a null
122	```
123	Outputs: `results/axis3_consistency.json`,
124	`results/axis3_self_consistency.tsv`, `results/axis3_cross_model.tsv`.
125	
126	## Cost
127	
128	Every run prints `[cost] ... ~$X`. A PDF task is ~$0.005–0.01 per judge; a video
129	task ~$0.02 (image context dominates). Whisper transcripts, frames, and audio
130	clips are cached under `eval/.cache/` so reruns are cheap. Only 6 of the 10 tasks
131	have a released gold deliverable, so Axis 1/3 run on those 6.
132	```
133	PDF gold:   private_detectives __57b2cdf2, __a46d5cd2 ; producers_and_directors __e4f664ea
134	video gold: film_and_video_editors __e222075d, __75401f7c
135	audio gold: audio_and_video_technicians __38889c3b
136	```
137

# Rubric scorecard — Axis 1 & Axis 3

Scripts that grade GDPval rubrics along **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)** and
**Axis 3 (consistency)** from the take-home spec, plus the judge harness, model
suite, and null-input generator they depend on.

```
eval/
  models.py            # judge-model registry (provider × capability × modality)
  llm.py               # OpenAI-compatible client (OpenAI + OpenRouter), cost tracking
  media.py             # ffmpeg frames / audio clips + Whisper transcription (cached)
  deliverable.py       # Bundle: flat file view over a deliverable, incl. zip members
  tools.py             # agent tools, gated by each model's modalities
  harness.py           # the judge: tool-using agent -> per-item [0,1] scorecard
  rubric.py            # rubric loading + normalization (handles penalty items)
  null_inputs.py       # generates do-nothing deliverables per task
  axis1_boundary.py    # Axis 1 runner
  axis3_consistency.py # Axis 3 runner
  results/             # *.json (full detail) + *.tsv (scorecard rows)
```

## Setup

```bash
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install openai pymupdf pillow        # plus the existing pandas/pyarrow/requests
brew install ffmpeg                       # frames, audio extraction
# .env must define OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENROUTER_API_KEY
python scripts/download_data.py          # if data/ isn't populated yet
```

## The judge suite (`models.py`)

Chosen cheap, and to vary three things so the axes are measurable:

| key | provider | model | modalities | role on the axes |
|-----|----------|-------|-----------|------------------|
| `gpt-mini` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-mini | text, image | strong vision anchor; cross-vendor |
| `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-nano | text, image | weak rung → capability ablation vs `gpt-mini` |
| `qwen-vl` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text, image | cheap mid judge; cross-vendor |
| `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text | **modality ablation**: same family, no vision |
| `gemini-lite` | OpenRouter | google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text, image, **audio** | the only judge that natively *hears* |
| `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text, image, audio | free 2nd native-audio judge for A/V tasks |

A judge without `audio` still reaches audio *content* through the
`transcribe_audio` tool (Whisper). Native-audio vs transcript-only on the same
deliverable is exactly the **modality-access** experiment. The
`MODALITY_LADDER = [qwen-text, qwen-vl, gemini-lite]` is the text → +vision →
+audio triple for that ablation.

## The harness (`harness.py`)

One model grades one deliverable against one rubric as a small tool-calling
agent. It is handed the file list (deliverable files plus read-only `reference`
inputs for comparison criteria), inspects with the tools its modalities allow,
then calls `submit_scorecard` with a per-item **verdict**:
`met | unmet | partial | unverifiable`. Safeguards: per-judgement tool-call
budget, per-turn flood cap, duplicate-call dedupe, forced scorecard on the last round.

To give the judge a *fair shot* (early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to
things it couldn't see, vs ~3% genuine gaps), the toolbox includes deterministic
fact tools so objective criteria are never guessed:

* `probe_media` — ffprobe ground truth: codec, exact resolution, fps, runtime,
  audio channels (kills "cannot determine codec/resolution/runtime").
* `file_facts` — extension/type, PDF page count, word count ("is a PDF", "<= 2 pages").
* `view_video_frames` — defaults to **one frame per scene cut** (distinct shots,
  for "includes a shot of X" / identifiable-face checks); pass `start/end` to
  re-sample a window at higher resolution to read on-screen text.
* read-only **reference files**, so "matches the script" items can compare.
* an explicit **`unverifiable`** verdict for criteria no judge can check from the
  artifact (external provenance like "footage is royalty-free / from Pexels").

Scores are normalized by the **positive ceiling** (sum of positive item points);
**penalty items** (negative points, e.g. "uses footage with identifiable faces:
-85") subtract when `met`, flooring at 0. Two scores are reported: `normalized`
(unverifiable items = 0) and `normalized_verifiable_only` (unverifiable items
dropped from numerator and denominator — the fair score given what is checkable).
A large gap between them is itself a rubric **verifiability defect**.

## Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)

Per task, keyed on the gold deliverable's type:

| gold type | `blank` | `unedited_input` |
|-----------|---------|------------------|
| PDF | one empty page | the unrevised source doc rendered to PDF |
| video (mp4) | 5 s black + silence | source audio over a black frame |
| archive (stems zip) | zip of 5 s silence | the raw input audio re-zipped, unmixed |

```bash
python -m eval.null_inputs          # (re)generate all; writes eval/null_inputs/<task>/<variant>/
```

## Axis 1 — boundary calibration

Runs the rubric on gold (expect ≈1) and on each null (expect ≈0). Reports the
gold−null **gap** and **leaks**: positive items a do-nothing output still passes
(form/presence criteria — reward-hacking suspects, feeds Axis 2).

```bash
python -m eval.axis1_boundary                          # all gold tasks, judge=gpt-mini
python -m eval.axis1_boundary --models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
python -m eval.axis1_boundary --tasks <folder> --verbose
```
Outputs: `results/axis1_boundary.{json,tsv}` (cols: task, model, n_items,
gold_score, gold_verifiable_only, n_unverifiable, null_worst, gap, n_leaks,
null_breakdown).

## Axis 3 — consistency

* **Self-consistency**: one judge, same deliverable, `--repeats` times at
  `--temperature` → score stdev + fraction of items with a unanimous binary call.
* **Cross-model**: a `--panel` of judges, once each → score spread, pairwise
  gaps, and the most-contested items (vague criteria to reword).

```bash
python -m eval.axis3_consistency                                   # defaults
python -m eval.axis3_consistency --self-model qwen-vl --repeats 5 \
    --panel gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
python -m eval.axis3_consistency --on unedited_input               # consistency on a null
```
Outputs: `results/axis3_consistency.json`,
`results/axis3_self_consistency.tsv`, `results/axis3_cross_model.tsv`.

## Cost

Every run prints `[cost] ... ~$X`. A PDF task is ~$0.005–0.01 per judge; a video
task ~$0.02 (image context dominates). Whisper transcripts, frames, and audio
clips are cached under `eval/.cache/` so reruns are cheap. Only 6 of the 10 tasks
have a released gold deliverable, so Axis 1/3 run on those 6.
```
PDF gold:   private_detectives __57b2cdf2, __a46d5cd2 ; producers_and_directors __e4f664ea
video gold: film_and_video_editors __e222075d, __75401f7c
audio gold: audio_and_video_technicians __38889c3b
```
